policy_text: The Change Management and Release Policy aligns with ITIL v4 principles.
The Change Management and Release Policy aligns with ISO/IEC 20000-1:2018 (IT Service Management) framework.
All changes must be systematically logged within the central enterprise ITSM platform (ServiceNow) under the Change_Request taxonomy.
Changes beyond the LAN port of the Customer Premises Equipment (CPE) are prohibited unless explicitly covered under a fully-managed LAN/WLAN contractual addendum.
The scope includes core network elements (Optical, IP-MPLS, 5G Core), enterprise mobility platforms (cAPN routers), SD-WAN Orchestrators, and outward-facing API Gateways.
The ISO/IEC 20000-1:2018 framework is audited annually across Pune and Mumbai Tier-III core operations centers.
Changes must follow standardized life cycle protocols to minimize unpredicted service outages that violate enterprise Service Level Agreements (SLAs).
Changes are classified into four tiers based on risk, scope, and urgency, dictating approval workflow and lead time requirements.
Standard (Pre-Approved) changes require a minimum lead time for execution.
The Change Advisory Board (CAB) and Emergency Change Advisory Board (eCAB) are defined as governance bodies for change approvals.
The ITSM platform (ServiceNow) is the authoritative source for all change records and workflows.
The policy applies to RFP Writers, Solutions Architects, NOC/SOC Engineers, and Enterprise Quality Auditors.
Activating a new SD-WAN branch logic template via zero-touch provisioning (ZTP) is auto-approved upon logging.
Updating non-core router firewall rules or modifying API rate-limit configurations requires peer review and has a 3-business-day minimum lead time.
Upgrading the firmware on the primary Mumbai SD-WAN Orchestrator cluster requires a 14-business-day minimum lead time.
Emergency changes to resolve Priority 1 (P1) incidents or mitigate zero-day security vulnerabilities must be executed as soon as possible (ASAP).
The Change Advisory Board (CAB) is the cross-functional gating authority for all Normal (Minor & Major) changes.
All Normal (Minor & Major) changes must be reviewed during the weekly CAB meeting held every Tuesday at 14:00 IST.
Technical feasibility must be verified by testing changes in the staging environment before CAB approval.
Risk assessment must evaluate the impact blast radius if a proposed change fails.
Conflict avoidance requires verifying that proposed maintenance windows do not overlap with major core upgrades or customer business-critical periods (e.g., e-commerce client during Diwali sales).
Standard (Pre-Approved) changes are defined as low-risk, highly documented, and frequently executed tasks with proven track records.
Normal (Minor) changes are defined as low-to-medium risk modifications that do not require dedicated outage windows but need peer review.
Normal (Major) changes are defined as high-risk modifications requiring scheduled outages or spanning multiple core nodes.
Emergency changes are defined as immediate modifications required to resolve Priority 1 (P1) incidents or mitigate zero-day security vulnerabilities.
Standard lead times are suspended when a P1 incident threatens to breach a 99.99% availability SLA.
The Incident Commander must initiate an eCAB bridge call for emergency changes.
Emergency changes require verbal approval from the National Head of Network Operations and the Enterprise CISO (or their designated duty deputies).
All emergency changes must have a formal RFC retrospectively logged in ServiceNow within 24 hours.
No Normal or Emergency change is authorized without a comprehensively documented and tested rollback plan.
The RFC must detail the exact commands or automation scripts required to revert the system to its pre-change state.
If a change execution breaches the allocated Maintenance Window by >15 minutes without successful completion, the execution engineer must abort and initiate the rollback sequence.
Core Infrastructure maintenance windows are Sundays between 01:00 IST and 05:00 IST.
Customer Edge (CPE) maintenance windows default to Saturdays between 23:00 IST and 03:00 IST unless negotiated per customer.
Normal (Major) Changes require automated portal notifications and email alerts at T-10 days, T-24 hours, and immediately upon MW completion.
Emergency Changes require out-of-band communication (SMS and dedicated AM calls) to affected customer technical contacts within 30 minutes of eCAB approval.
ISO 20000 and SOC 2 Type II audit requirements must be satisfied through strict digital chain-of-custody rules.
The engineer executing the change cannot be the same engineer who authored the RFC or approved the CAB ticket.
Automated pre-change and post-change configuration snapshots must be archived immutably in central Rancid/Oxidized repositories.
Changes executed by OEM partners (e.g., Cisco, Fortinet resident engineers) on behalf of VI Business must strictly follow the internal SOP.
Configurations altered by enterprise admins via the VI Self-Care portal (e.g., updating IoT SIM APNs) are classified as 'Standard Changes' and bypass CAB, with implicit customer risk acceptance.
When addressing 'How do you ensure stability during upgrades?', cite Section 4 (CAB Process) and Section 6 (Rollback Expectations) to demonstrate rigorous gating.
Planned Maintenance Windows, as defined in Section 7, are explicitly excluded from SLA uptime calculations and penalty formulas.
The National Head of ITSM is designated as the owner of the Change Management and Release Policy.
The Operations Head and CEBO are designated as reviewers of the Change Management and Release Policy.
The Change Management and Release Policy must be updated annually, aligned with post-audit remediation cycles.

policy_text: The Delivery Resource Capability Matrix formalizes technical competencies, experiential bands, geographic distribution, and deployment methodology of 10,000+ technical engineering resources.
The document supports Request for Proposals (RFPs) demanding detailed profiles of personnel administering SD-WAN, Core IP Networks, Managed Security (SOC), and Enterprise Mobility Services.
All resources are direct employees of Vodafone Idea Limited (VIL) unless explicitly denoted as an 'Authorized Ecosystem Partner'.
'Shift Coverage' refers to standard 24x7x365 support mechanisms anchored in the Pune and Mumbai Tier-III core operations centers.
The document is applicable to Bid Writers, Solution Architects (SA), HR Auditors, and Customer Success Managers (CSM).
The scope includes operations and engineering staff directly supporting external enterprise managed service contracts.
The technical delivery organization is strictly layered to ensure complex faults reach specialized engineers rapidly, bypassing administrative buffers.
L1 NOC Incident Analyst must hold CCNA and ITIL v4 Foundation certifications.
L1 NOC Incident Analyst is capable of alert triaging, basic diagnostics, and carrier escalation.
L1 NOC Incident Analyst can resolve Layer 1/2 faults, basic link flapping, and automated ticket enrichment.
L2 NOC Network Engineer must hold CCNP (Enterprise/SP) and Fortinet NSE 4 certifications.
L2 NOC Network Engineer is capable of BGP/OSPF routing and Orchestrator tuning.
L2 NOC Network Engineer can resolve complex routing anomalies, QoS restructuring, and SD-WAN policy creation.
L3 Network Architect (SME) must hold CCIE, JNCIE, and AWS Advanced Networking certifications.
L3 Network Architect (SME) is capable of core protocol design and global failover.
L3 Network Architect (SME) can resolve zero-day bug resolution, bespoke architecture modifications, and crisis management during P1s.
The company employs over 1,200 specialized field-support engineers across 28 states and 8 Union Territories.
Field engineers must access >90% of Indian pincodes within a 4-hour window for urban areas and 8-hour window for rural areas during catastrophic hardware replacement.
Pune and Mumbai operations teams must operate on a 24x7x365 rotating shift basis with no out-of-hours 'on-call' lag for enterprise fault resolution.
Primary language support for the Enterprise Service Desk must include Business English and Hindi.
Regional language support must be available in 12 localized Indian languages during standard business hours upon specialized routing request.
Core intelligence and monitoring platforms must be exclusively governed by direct VI Business personnel.
Ecosystem partners may only perform 'Hands and Feet' hardware unboxing, cabling, and rack mounting at customer premises.
Ecosystem partners must perform local civil-works digging and immediate fiber-splicing post-cut under strict mean-time-to-repair SLAs.
All ecosystem partners must sign strict non-disclosure agreements (NDAs) mirroring core VIL compliance posture.
Ecosystem partners must be subject to immediate VI Business disciplinary auditing.
CV/Resume requests in RFPs must reference Section 3 to explain dynamic team rotation for 24x7 coverage.
Quality Assurance must emphasize Section 4 (Experience Bands) to justify premium delivery vs. low-cost integrators.
Governance ownership must be assigned to the National Head of IT Service Delivery.
Governance reviewers must include HR Operations, Head of Network Operations, and Enterprise CISO.
Governance updates must be validated annually with certification counts extracted from the HR management portal before major Q1 audits.
The Field Operations Capability Document serves as the internal baseline for answering RFP sections pertaining to 'Site Installations,' 'Hardware Replacement (RMA),' and 'Local Support Capabilities'.

VI Business (India) Field Engineering teams execute complex, multi-site enterprise deployments (e.g., nationwide SD-WAN rollouts) and maintain physical hardware SLAs in dense urban hubs and remote rural territories.
All deployment and break-fix SLAs assume customer sites are safely accessible by road and free from acute regional civil disturbances or natural disasters (force majeure).
Target resolution times for SLAs begin only after the central Pune Tier-III NOC completes remote diagnostics and officially dispatches a field ticket.
Site survey capability mandates empirical feasibility checks before complex physical fiber builds or multi-floor Wi-Fi deployments.
Desktop Feasibility checks using internal GIS mapping overlays must validate optical fiber proximity within 4 hours of an Account Manager's query.
Physical Site Surveys must be dispatched for sites requiring new civil works or Last-Mile fiber trenching.
For Physical Fiber Cut faults, on-site response SLAs are 4 hours (Urban/Tier 1) and 8 hours (Rural/Tier 3) after OTDR distance confirmation via NOC.
For Complete CPE Hard-Down faults, on-site response SLAs are Next Business Day (NBD) for both Urban/Tier 1 and Rural/Tier 3 sites after power/hardware failure confirmation.
For Degraded Performance faults, on-site response SLAs are 48 hours (Urban/Tier 1) and 72 hours (Rural/Tier 3) after persistent packet loss confirmation.
Hardware Staging: CPE is procured and centrally staged in the VI Mumbai Logistics Hub, where its serial number is mapped to the customer’s cloud orchestrator tenant.
Unconfigured hardware is shipped directly to the remote site via secure national courier partners.
A localized VI Ecosystem Partner (certified technician) must physically unbox, mount, and connect the device.
Logical provisioning requires the device to dial the central Orchestrator, download its configuration, and come online without local CLI configuration.
No local command-line interface (CLI) configuration is permitted during logical provisioning.
For Physical Fiber Cut faults, on-site response SLA is 4 hours in urban/Tier 1 locations and 8 hours in rural/Tier 3 locations.
For Complete CPE Hard-Down faults, on-site response SLA is Next Business Day (NBD) for both urban/Tier 1 and rural/Tier 3 locations.
Field Operations dispatch is triggered only when remote NOC troubleshooting fails to restore a down device.
Dispatch for Physical Fiber Cut faults requires OTDR distance confirmation via NOC as a trigger condition.
VI Business maintains a decentralized warehouse topology to ensure hardware replacement SLAs are viable across India's vast geography.
The National Distribution Center (NDC) in Bhiwandi (Maharashtra) manages bulk OEM imports and central staging.
Regional Spares Depots (RSD) in 16 major capital cities stock 'hot spares' for standard Fortinet/Cisco SD-WAN routers, enabling Next Business Day (NBD) dispatch.
4-Hour CPE replacement requires a dedicated, premium 'Forward Stocking Location' (FSL) contract.

The RMA workflow requires the RSD to ship replacement devices to the site, with the local Field Engineer swapping the device, confirming ZTP logic restoration with the NOC, and packaging the faulty unit for reverse logistics.

VI Business maintains the densest enterprise field support footprint in the Indian telecom market, with 1,200+ localized Field Engineers handling core optical splicing, cell-tower maintenance, and complex enterprise switching.
A vetted network of 5,000+ certified 'Smart Hands' technicians extends VI Business's reach into Tier-3 and Tier-4 townships.
VI Business ensures physical response capabilities across all 28 states and 8 Union Territories, supporting >95% of active Indian postal codes.

All field interventions require engineers to upload timestamped, geo-tagged photographs (e.g., neat rack cabling, lit interface LEDs) to the Field Service Management app before the ticket can be closed.
If an on-site engineer cannot resolve an anomaly within 2 hours of arrival, the ticket automatically escalates to the Regional Field Operations Manager and bridges L3 Subject Matter Experts in Pune.
Customers are strictly responsible for providing stable Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) and clear physical access to the server room.
Field SLAs are paused if the engineer is denied entry by local customer security.
Fiber restoration SLAs (e.g., 4 hours) are dependent on local police or municipal authorities granting immediate permission to excavate and splice.
For banking or retail clients with hundreds of remote rural branches, Section 4 (ZTP Workflow) and Section 7 (Coverage Depth) must be forcefully cited to address deployment concerns.
Distinguish between 'NOC Response Time' (15 minutes) and 'Physical On-Site Response Time' (Section 5) to avoid overcommitting in commercial contracts.
The National Head of Field Operations is designated as the owner of governance and ownership.
The Head of IT Service Delivery and CEBO are designated as reviewers for governance and ownership.
Governance and ownership documentation must be reviewed annually, corresponding with logistics partner contract renewals.
External extracts from this document are permitted.

The Information Security Policies Set is certified under ISO/IEC 27001:2022.
The policies are non-negotiable and deviations require explicit, minuted Deal Desk and CISO approval.
All encryption algorithms must adhere to the minimum cryptographic standards endorsed by the Government of India (MeitY).
The policies apply to Bid Writers, Security Architects, Legal Compliance Teams, and enterprise auditors reviewing Security Questionnaires.
The policies cover Mumbai and Pune Tier-III operations centers, centralized cloud orchestrators, and corporate end-user devices accessing production networks.
Engineering access to customer tenant configurations (e.g., SD-WAN VRFs) is denied by default and requires Just-In-Time (JIT) provisioning tied to an approved ServiceNow incident or change ticket.
Access to the core IP network, API gateways, and internal CRM/Billing portals requires mandatory physical token or authenticator app MFA for all internal and partner identities.
Access to Tier-III data centers requires dual-factor biometric (retina/fingerprint) plus RFID badge authentication.
Tier-III data centers are safeguarded by 24x7x365 armed perimeter security and 90-day CCTV retention.
All corporate laptops issued to Level 2/Level 3 engineers operate on a zero-trust architecture. Local admin rights are strictly stripped.
USB mass storage devices are hard-disabled via Group Policy Object (GPO). Enterprise file-sharing is enforced exclusively through audited, encrypted OneDrive instances.
All endpoints enforce an always-on Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) agent monitored directly by the internal Pune SOC.
Vulnerability and port-scanning engines sweep the external IP perimeters, API gateways, and core orchestration clusters continuously across a 7-day rolling window.
Formal, grey-box penetration testing is conducted bi-annually by an independent, CERT-In empanelled third-party auditor.
Critical 'Zero-Day' vulnerabilities (CVSS >9.0) must be contained or mitigated within 4 hours of publication.
Non-critical vulnerabilities are triaged monthly.

Critical patches mitigating active exploitation threats bypass standard Change Advisory Board (CAB) lead times. They are tested immediately in a segmented sandbox and pushed to production within 24 hours via the eCAB process.

Standard patches (Firmware/OS) are scheduled monthly and applied uniformly across redundant active-standby clusters to ensure zero downtime to customer edge routing during the Maintenance Window (MW).
Core network elements reaching vendor EoSL are proactively scheduled for total hardware replacement prior to support expiration.
All security events, firewall drops, API authentication failures, and BGP state changes are forcefully syslog-forwarded to a centralized, immutable Splunk/QRadar SIEM cluster hosted in Pune.

Raw traffic telemetry and authentication logs are retained in encrypted cold storage (AWS ap-south-1) for a minimum of 12 rolling months to satisfy the Indian Department of Telecommunications (DoT) lawful interception and forensics mandates.
Engineering staff, including senior L3 SMEs, completely lack the permission to modify, delete, or truncate system event logs.
All unboxed Customer Premises Equipment (CPE), specifically uCPE routers and firewalls, undergo a mandatory secure-baseline provisioning template wipe prior to leaving the Bhiwandi logistics hub.
Factory default admin credentials are programmatically overwritten prior to deployment. Devices failing initialization checks are immediately rejected and quarantined.
100% of VI Business (India) employees must complete an interactive Cyber Security Awareness module annually. Failure to complete results in automatic revocation of internal network access.

Deliberate circumvention of the Access Control Policy or MFA protocols is classified as Gross Misconduct, leading to immediate suspension and potential civil litigation under the Information Technology Act, 2000.

These policies enforce the VI Business managed perimeter. If a customer connects unmanaged, vulnerable devices to their LAN port behind the VI-managed SD-WAN edge, those local network segment risks fall squarely upon the customer's internal CISO.
Treat this document as the 'Rosetta Stone' for answering massive Excel-based security questionnaires by lifting exact claims from Section 3 to address specific controls.
Cite 'ISO 27001:2022 Certification' as external proof of adherence to policies when questioned by auditors.

The Insurance Coverage Summary outlines corporate liability, cyber risk, and physical asset insurance shields maintained by Vodafone Idea Limited (VIL) for VI Business (India) enterprise operations.

Insurance policies are maintained at the consolidated VIL corporate level. Distinct, separate policies are not purchased per individual enterprise customer contract unless explicitly priced and approved by the Enterprise Deal Desk.
All insurance policy limits are quoted in Indian Rupees (INR) or US Dollars (USD) where global reinsurance treaties apply, converted at the prevailing RBI reference rate.
Target audience for this document includes Enterprise Legal Reviewers, RFP Compliance Teams, and Contract Negotiators.
Scope of insurance coverage applies to all managed network, IoT, and SOC contracts executed under standard Master Services Agreements (MSA).

Commercial General Liability (CGL) coverage protects against claims for third-party bodily injury and tangible property damage occurring on customer premises due directly to the negligence of VI Business personnel or authorized field ecosystem partners.
Professional Indemnity (Errors & Omissions - E&O) coverage is maintained as part of the insurance portfolio.
Insurance policies are backed by Tier-1 Indian and global underwriters.
source_section: 3. Core Insurance Types and Coverage Scope

policy_text: Covers financial losses strictly from proven failure of VI Business to perform telecom/IT services as defined in signed BoM and SLA.
Excludes coverage for breaches originating from customer's own insecure LAN or compromised edge endpoints.
Commercial General Liability has an aggregate annual limit of INR 250 Crores and per occurrence limit of INR 50 Crores.
Professional Indemnity (E&O) has an aggregate annual limit of USD 50 Million and per occurrence limit of USD 10 Million.
Cyber Liability has an aggregate annual limit of USD 25 Million and per occurrence limit of USD 5 Million.
Workmen’s Compensation adheres strictly to the Workmen’s Compensation Act, 1923 (India).
Managed SD-WAN & MPLS are covered by E&O for configuration faults and CGL for physical installation risks.
Security Operations (SOC) are exclusively covered under Cyber Liability for missed IOCs leading to proven breaches, subject to forensic limitations.

The Migration and Cutover Playbook must codify exact technical and operational sequencing for enterprise network migrations with zero unplanned business disruption.
Cutover is defined as the specific maintenance window where traffic shifts from the legacy network to the VI Business network.
Hypercare is defined as an elevated, dedicated support period immediately following a Cutover.
A Go/No-Go Call is a mandatory governance bridge attended by VI and Customer stakeholders immediately prior to Cutover execution.
Maintenance Windows (MW) are restricted to Saturday 23:00 IST to Sunday 04:00 IST for standard cutovers to minimize active business impact.
Fallback requires legacy competitor circuits to remain active and billed during the coexistence phase until the VI SD-WAN is fully stabilized.
The Migration and Cutover Playbook must demonstrate a battle-tested, ITIL-aligned methodology to mitigate deployment risk for C-Suite evaluators.
All items on the Readiness Log must be verified as 'GREEN' before a site is scheduled for Cutover.
Customer Premises Equipment (CPE) must be physically racked, powered, and confirmed active by the local Field Engineer prior to Cutover.
New VI primary optical circuits and secondary LTE/Internet circuits must be in place before Cutover execution.
The Transition Management organization must follow an ITIL-aligned methodology for enterprise network migrations.
The Migration and Cutover Playbook applies to large-scale WAN migrations, SIP Trunking portability, and central firewall replacements.

BGP peering toward the customer LAN must be staged in an 'Admin Down' state.
The customer CISO must approve the new firewall rule-base governing Internet access.
VI Business prohibits 'Big Bang' migrations (switching all 5,000 branches simultaneously) for enterprise environments.
An Alpha Pilot (2-5 Sites) must be conducted at low-risk, non-critical locations to validate staging logic, ZTP download times, and shipping logistics.
A Beta Pilot (10-25 Sites) must include at least one medium-complexity site to validate Coexistence routing logic.
The Beta Pilot must survive a 14-day Hypercare period without P1/P2 incidents before the customer CIO signs the 'Mass Rollout Authority' document.
During a 12-month nationwide migration, 'Migrated Sites' must seamlessly communicate with 'Legacy Sites'.

policy_text: The Command Center must be staffed by a VI Transition Manager, VI L3 Network SME, Local Smart-Hands Field Engineer, and Customer Network Operations Lead.
The Go/No-Go decision must be made at T-00:00 by polling all stakeholders.
The customer must shut down their legacy LAN-facing interface (Administrative Down) at T+00:05.
The VI Field Engineer must patch the Customer LAN switch into the new VI SD-WAN CPE at T+00:10.
At T+00:15, the VI NOC brings the BGP neighbor 'Up' and the new VI network learns local LAN subnets, advertising them globally via the Orchestrator.
At T+00:30, Customer IT must run pre-defined ping and application-login tests against their core ERP system.
At T+00:45, if tests are successful, the Commander declares 'Stable Cutover' and the site enters Hypercare.
If application-login tests fail at T+00:30 and the VI NOC cannot identify the root cause within 45 minutes, a mandatory Rollback is declared.
During Rollback, the VI Field Engineer must physically repatch the LAN cable back into the legacy competitor router to restore traffic.
After a Rollback, the site is removed from the rollout schedule pending a formal Root Cause Analysis (RCA) delivered by the VI Change Advisory Board (CAB) within 48 hours.
For 7 calendar days post-cutover, any alert from the newly cutover site must ring directly to the Hypercare Tiger Team (L2/L3 engineers).
The site transitions to standard BAU NOC support only after maintaining 7 continuous days without P1 or P2 configuration-related incidents.
Migrations may stall due to legacy vendor hostility; the Customer retains ultimate responsibility for compelling the incumbent vendor to adhere to the Coexistence timeline.
When answering 'Transition Plan' RFPs, cite Section 5 (Coexistence NNI) and Section 7 (Rollback Plan) as technical proofs to win transition bids.
When defending 'slow' timelines, use Section 4 (Pilot Approach) to explain why rushing migrations endangers core business applications.

The Network Operations Manual provides the definitive structural and procedural blueprint governing the VI Business (India) Tier-III Network Operations Center (NOC) and associated field dispatch teams.
The NOC is located in Pune (Primary) and Mumbai (DR).
Incident response metrics assume the customer has procured a proactive managed service tier, enabling the VI NOC to open tickets autonomously upon SNMP trap/Syslog generation.
The target audience for this document includes NOC Engineers, Service Delivery Managers, and Bid Writers answering 'Support Operations' queries.
The scope of this document includes Managed SD-WAN, Enterprise MPLS Core, and Managed IoT routing domains.
L1 Incident Analysts must acknowledge alerts within 15 minutes, perform basic circuit ping/traceroute tests, log incidents in ServiceNow, and engage local exchange teams for fiber cuts.
L2 Network Engineers must hold certificated routing expertise (e.g., CCNP) to handle BGP routing anomalies, SD-WAN policy creation, and complex asymmetric path troubleshooting.
L3 Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) are the escalation point for zero-day bugs, overarching systemic degradation, and emergency Change Request (eCAB) authorizations.
Incident Managers (Major Incident Team) are activated only during P1 (Critical) severity events to manage customer communication and bridge orchestration.
VI Business uses a carrier-grade OSS stack to enforce proactive stability.
Fault & Event Management uses IBM Netcool/SolarWinds to consolidate millions of daily SNMP traps into correlated, actionable alarms.
ITSM (Ticketing) uses ServiceNow as the single source of truth for all Incident (INC), Change (CHG), and Problem (PRB) records.
SD-WAN Orchestration uses CISCO vManage/FortiManager for centralized cloud management, ZTP staging, and policy enforcement.
Traffic Analytics uses Kentik/Arbor Peakflow for real-time netflow analysis to detect DDoS attacks and bandwidth saturation.
Proactive polling of core network elements and Customer Premises Equipment (CPE) occurs via SNMPv3 every 60 seconds.
CPE missing 3 consecutive polling cycles (3 minutes) triggers an alert triage sequence.
Netcool correlates alerts and checks for scheduled Maintenance Windows (MW) during alert triage.
If no Maintenance Window exists, Netcool auto-generates a ServiceNow P2 Incident during alert triage.
L1 Analysts must acknowledge ServiceNow tickets within 15 minutes during alert triage (SLA: 15 mins).
Maintenance Windows (MW) are checked by Netcool during alert correlation to determine incident generation.

The NOC retains overarching ownership of the ticket for all incidents.
Field Engineers cannot close a P1 ticket; they must request the NOC to verify BGP restoration and payload passage before closure is authorized.
For any P1 or P2 incident lasting longer than 4 hours, an automated Problem Management workflow triggers.
An Initial RCA (RFO - Reason for Outage) must be provided to the customer within 4 hours of incident closure.
A Comprehensive RCA must be delivered within 3 Business Days using the 5-Whys methodology.
Daily shift-handover bridges occur at 08:00 IST and 20:00 IST, lasting exactly 30 minutes.
The Problem Management review board meets weekly on Wednesdays at 14:00 IST to categorize repeating P3 incidents.
Monthly capacity planning reviews are conducted by L3 SMEs using Kentik trending graphs.
For customer-managed devices under the 'Wires Only' model, the NOC's diagnostic capability halts at the optical hand-off.
The NOC cannot provide RCA on customer-owned edge firewalls.

policy_text: Typical scale for campus environments is 5 to 20 massive campus environments housing 5,000+ simultaneous FTEs.
Design pattern for campus environments uses 'Massive Throughput Hub-and-Spoke' with dual or triple optical fiber rings terminating on disjointed core POPs.
Underlay for campus environments scales port capacities from 1 Gbps to 10 Gbps entirely on high-SLA ILL circuits.
Overlay for campus environments uses direct peering to hyperscalers (AWS Direct Connect / Azure ExpressRoute) with BGP and no SD-WAN overhead at the branch level.
Security overlay for campus environments requires customer-managed edge firewalls ('Wires Only') and VI Business-provided DDoS scrubbing (Arbor Peakflow) in the cloud.
Compliance requirement for international clients includes ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II assurance.
Typical scale for manufacturing and heavy industry is 50 to 150 sprawling manufacturing plants plus centralized corporate HQ.
Design pattern for manufacturing uses 'Resilient Ruggedized Edge + IoT' with primary 50 Mbps ILL and secondary 4G/LTE Enterprise Mobility SIMs as active backup.
Overlay for manufacturing uses SD-WAN to ensure seamless LTE failover during fiber cuts.
IoT integration for manufacturing routes smart-metering and fleet telemetry data through a private Custom APN (cAPN) directly into the customer's on-premise ERP, bypassing the public internet.
Security overlay for manufacturing deploys edge firewalls (uCPE) to segment corporate IT networks from factory-floor OT networks.
Outcome positioning for manufacturing is 'Preventing assembly line halts due to fiber cuts via invisible LTE failover.'
Typical scale for retail and QSR is 5,000 to 10,000 highly dynamic, small-footprint storefronts.
Design pattern for retail uses 'Rapid LTE Primary Edge' with primary 4G/LTE or commodity broadband and small branch routers (e.g., FortiGate 40F).
Overlay for retail uses SD-WAN to aggregate cheap underlays and guarantee PoS transaction speed.
Retail deployments use Zero-Touch Provisioning (ZTP) for rapid 'Store-in-a-Box' deployment within 48 hours.
Security overlay for retail uses a centralized Orchestrator to push unified Web Filtering policies to block streaming media on retail store Wi-Fi.
Outcome positioning for retail is 'Reducing time-to-revenue by opening new retail locations in hours, not months.'
SIP Voice Portability integrates 10,000+ simultaneous SIP Trunking channels (G.711/G.729) into IP-PBX or Contact Center platforms.

Risk assessments must be conducted as a continuous lifecycle process, not as one-time project phases.
The RMF must manage risks within the Board-approved corporate risk appetite, acknowledging that zero-risk is mathematically impossible in telecommunications.
The ALARP principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) must be applied to reduce risks to an acceptable level.
Residual risk must be explicitly defined as the threat level remaining after mitigation controls are applied.
The RMF must provide auditable methodologies to prevent catastrophic SLA breaches in Managed Network and Security portfolios.
Risks must be categorized and tracked within the ServiceNow Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) module.
Technical/Operational risks include hardware end-of-life (EoL), unpatched vulnerabilities, and catastrophic fiber cuts on non-redundant rings.
Commercial/Financial risks include currency fluctuations impacting OEM hardware imports and massive SLA penalty triggers.
Regulatory/Compliance risks include changes to DoT (Department of Telecommunications) OSP routing rules.
The RMF must serve as proof of formal risk governance to enterprise Chief Risk Officers (CROs).
The RMF must apply to Managed Network Service deployments, SOC operations, and massive CAPEX infrastructure upgrades.
DPDP Act 2023 breach fines
Supply chain delays for active Cisco/Fortinet hardware holding up a 5,000-site deployment
Risks are mathematically scored using a standardized 5x5 Probability vs. Impact matrix
Score range 1-6: Low (Green) - Accept Risk. Monitor quarterly
Score range 8-12: Medium (Amber) - Mitigate. Implement controls within 90 days
Score range 15-20: High (Red) - Mitigate Immediately. Weekly C-Suite review
Score range 25: Critical (Black) - Cease Operations. Immediate CEO intervention
When a risk scores 'Medium' or above, the designated Risk Owner must select a formal containment strategy: Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, or Accept

The SOC must categorize alerts into a triage severity matrix within 15 minutes of detection.
S1 (Critical) incidents require an initial response within 15 minutes.

The SIRP does not apply to unmanaged customer-owned LAN segments or user endpoints.
Time-to-contain SLAs for Zero-Day vulnerabilities are considered 'Best Effort' and not guaranteed.

The SOC must monitor and parse millions of daily SIEM logs for threat detection.
Active, confirmed lateral movement is classified as an S1 (Critical) incident.
Ransomware detonation is classified as an S1 (Critical) incident.
Core network compromise is classified as an S1 (Critical) incident.
Verified external exploit attempt against a known unpatched perimeter vulnerability is classified as an S1 (Critical) incident.
Phishing campaign yielding credential theft is classified as an S1 (Critical) incident.
The initial response SLA for S1 (Critical) incidents is 15 minutes.
The escalation authority for S1 (Critical) incidents is the Enterprise CISO.
Malware detected and auto-quarantined by endpoint EDR is classified as an S3 (Medium) incident.
Policy violations (e.g., unauthorized scanning) are classified as S3 (Medium) incidents.
The initial response SLA for S3 (Medium) incidents is 2 hours.
The escalation authority for S3 (Medium) incidents is the Head of SOC Operations.
Generic threat intelligence feed noise is classified as an S4 (Low) incident.
Suppressed port-scan attempts dropped by edge firewall are classified as S4 (Low) incidents.
The initial response SLA for S4 (Low) incidents is the next business day.
The escalation authority for S4 (Low) incidents is the L1 SOC Analyst.
The Incident Commander (CISO/Head of SOC) retains absolute authority over containment strategy during S1/S2 incidents.
The Incident Commander authorizes emergency, service-disrupting firewall drops during S1/S2 incidents.
Incident Handlers (Tier 2/3 Threat Hunters) execute technical forensic analysis during S1/S2 incidents.
The Communications Lead (Customer Success) is the exclusive point of contact for crisis communication with the affected Customer IT team.
Engineers are prohibited from communicating unverified technical hypotheses directly to the customer during an incident.
The CSIRT uses SIEM heuristics, EDR behavioral triggers, or external Threat Intel feeds (e.g., FireEye, CrowdStrike) to flag anomalous behavior.
L2 Analysts must verify flagged events to confirm they are genuine positives and not system misconfigurations.

Short-term containment requires isolating infected subnets or SD-WAN branches via physical disconnection or strict "Deny-All" VRF rules at the core to prevent lateral spread, even if it causes temporary branch downtime.
Long-term containment requires rebuilding active directories, rotating all Service Account credentials, and enforcing emergency out-of-band firmware patching.
Deep forensic scans must be conducted to determine the exact method of perimeter breach by the threat actor.
Systems must be patched, tested in a segregated sandbox, and gradually brought back online to monitor for resurgence.
Digital evidence must comply with the Indian IT Act 2000 to survive scrutiny in Indian courts of law.
Bit-for-bit, write-blocked hard drive images must be created for compromised uCPE or core servers before initiating eradication.
Firewall transit logs and authentication attempts must be hashed (SHA-256) and stored in encrypted AWS S3 cold storage to prevent post-breach tampering.
A physical/digital custody log must be maintained to record who accessed forensic payloads and when.
For S1/S2 incidents impacting customer payload or tenant orchestrators, the VI Communications Lead must notify the Customer's CISO/Security Lead within 2 hours of verified identification.

The WAN Performance Telemetry API (/network/sdwan/v1/telemetry) extrapolates latency, jitter, and packet loss metrics across primary (Optical) and secondary (LTE/5G) links.
The Bandwidth on Demand (BoD) API (/network/mpls/v1/bod) allows dynamic scaling of bandwidth for designated MPLS ports up to 2x commit on an hourly billing model.
The SIM Lifecycle Management API (/iot/v2/sim/lifecycle) enables activation, suspension, or termination of SIM/eSIM profiles on the VI network.
The Data Usage Analytics API (/iot/v2/usage/metrics) retrieves real-time aggregate and per-device granular data consumption metrics.

The Training and Adoption Framework establishes a structured curriculum to ensure enterprise customers can autonomously operate and derive maximum ROI from newly deployed managed services.
A formal, KPI-driven enablement phase is built directly into the Project Plan to guarantee customer IT staff and end-users understand new platforms.
Adoption KPIs track how deeply customers utilize advanced features (e.g., creating custom reporting dashboards in Splunk).
Train-the-Trainer (TTT) methodology is used for massive user scaling.
A Learning Management System (LMS) is utilized for training delivery.
Training is localized to the Indian context using fluent English/Hindi engineering trainers, not generic offshore recorded webinars.
Customer IT staff must have basic technical competency (e.g., understanding IP subnetting) before attending technical training.
Target audience for training includes Customer Success Managers (CSM), Project Managers, and Bid Writers.
Training scope is limited to Managed Network Services (Orchestrator Management), Cybersecurity (SIEM/Firewall Dashboards), and Unified Communications (UCaaS).
Role-based enablement is required, rejecting generic 'one-size-fits-all' training.
Executive/C-Suite training includes a 60-minute briefing on interpreting Monthly SLA Reports, calculating ROI, and navigating executive dashboards.
IT Administrators (L1/L2) receive a 4-8 hour technical workshop covering fault triage, API reports, firewall object groups, and emergency rollback procedures.
End Users (UCaaS/Voice) receive mass adoption training via Train-the-Trainer methodology focused on SIP/Teams softphones, call transfers, and voicemail configuration.
Instructor-Led, Virtual (Standard) training sessions must be conducted by a certified VI Business Subject Matter Expert (SME).
Instructor-Led, Virtual (Standard) sessions must be strictly recorded and MP4 files transferred to the customer's LMS for future onboarding.
Hypercare post-migration shadowing must occur for the first 72 hours, with a senior VI SOC/NOC engineer guiding the customer IT lead.
On-Site Instructor-Led (Premium) training is reserved for TCV deals exceeding INR 10 Crore.
On-Site Instructor-Led (Premium) training requires a VI SME to embed physically in the customer's NOC for 3 business days.
Knowledge Transfer (KT) Plan must follow a strict 3-phase curriculum with defined content focus, deliverables, and timing.
Phase 1 of the KT Plan must deliver a signed LLD Document accepted by the Customer Architect 14 days pre-Go Live.
Phase 2 of the KT Plan must deliver an Administrator Certification of Completion 3 days post-Hypercare.
Phase 3 of the KT Plan must deliver a Final "KT Sign-off" Document executed after Advanced Optimization.
All training and KT activities must adhere to the specified timing constraints (e.g., T-14 Days, T+3 Days).
VI Business provides a localized collateral pack immediately following technical training, including Quick Reference Guides (QRGs) for P1 Escalation Matrix and helpdesk routing numbers.
VI Business delivers Custom Video Snippets (2-minute MP4s) demonstrating repetitive tasks like IP whitelisting in the Firewall Portal.
VI Business provides API Documentation detailing REST/JSON payload structures for integrating VI Service Portal telemetry into a customer's ServiceNow instance.
If a customer's IT Director does not log into the SD-WAN Orchestrator for 45 consecutive days, an automated 'Adoption Risk' alert is triggered for the Account Manager.
Self-Service Ticket Deflection tracks the percentage of L1 password-reset or basic reporting queries resolved via the portal without contacting the VI helpdesk.
Quarterly Business Reviews (QBRs) are conducted where VI CSMs review Adoption KPIs and propose 'Refresher Training' if metrics indicate platform underutilization.
Standard training is delivered in English/Hindi and uses generic, anonymized data sets for demonstrations.
Bespoke training modules requiring corporate branding and live production data require a standalone Professional Services engagement.
Section 3 (Role-Based Enablement) may be copied for RFPs to demonstrate understanding of training differences between roles.
